questions.question: What is hypertension and what can it be caused by:
questions.explanation: Hypertension refers to elevated blood pressure levels, which increase the workload on the heart and arteries and can lead to serious health issues.

questions.question: Flying at 5,000ft on a cold winter day when you suddenly feel a headache and your performance is increasingly on the decline, what might you be experiencing?
The average number of items that can be held in the short-term memory is:
questions.explanation: The average number of items that can be held in the short-term memory is approximately 7, plus or minus 2.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.14.1(b)(vi) memory limitations

questions.question: A symptom of carbon monoxide poisoning is:
options: Blue colouring of the fingers, lips and toes
warm and sluggish feelings with possible headaches

questions.question: What negative G loading is considered an acceptable limit for a healthy person?
questions.explanation: Negative 3 G is the typical acceptable limit for negative G loading in a healthy individual. Negative Gs (inverted or “eyeballs out” forces) are typically less well tolerated than positive Gs.

questions.question: Which scenario is most likely to lead to carbon monoxide poisoning?
options: Flying in the vicinity of thick smoke
a leak in the engine exhaust system
questions.explanation: A leak in the engine exhaust system can allow carbon monoxide to enter the cockpit, leading to poisoning.

questions.question: An effect of sustained negative G loading can:
options: allow excess blood to pool in the lower parts of the body

questions.question: What is the most effective countermeasure for combating fatigue during flight operations?
questions.explanation: Scheduled rest periods are the most effective countermeasure for combating fatigue during flight operations.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.14.4(c)(i) sleep management

questions.question: Which of the following trait that is considered a hazardous attitude which can adversely affect pilot decision-making?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.14.1(b)(i) personality traits, for example introvert/extrovert

questions.question: Hypoxia is most likely to impair a pilots performance at altitudes above:
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.13.1(c) know the symptoms are diffucult to detect in healthy individuals and can develop much faster at higher altitudes

questions.question: What is the main function of the vestibular system in flight?
questions.explanation: The vestibular system senses motion and orientation, helping pilots maintain balance and spatial orientation during flight.

questions.question: Stress in pilots can lead to:
What is the primary goal of Threat and Error Management (TEM)?
options: To eliminate all threats and errors
To identify and manage threats and errors before they lead to undesired aircraft states
questions.explanation: TEM aims to detect and handle threats and errors effectively to maintain safe flight operations.

questions.question: A 'threat' in the conquestion of TEM is best described as:
options: An external event or error that increases operational complexity
A mechanical failure caused by human error

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an 'undesired aircraft state'?
options: A pilot incorrectly programming the flight management system
An aircraft being on final approach with incorrect flap configuration
Weather briefing not being updated prior to flight

questions.question: Effective crew resource management (CRM) supports TEM by:
options: Reducing the need for standard operating procedures
Promoting teamwork and communication to manage threats and errors

questions.question: Which of the following actions is part of error management?
options: Ignoring the error to prevent confusion
identifying and trapping the error before it leads to consequences
Assuming errors are inevitable and uncontrollable

questions.question: At 30,000ft, the approximate time of useful consciousness is:
A runway that slopes up and away from the approach threshold can likely lead to the pilot to:
options: making a shallow approach and land short
making a steep approach and land short
making a steep approach and land long
making a shallow approach and land long
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.12(d)(ii) width and slope of runway

questions.question: Decompression sickness is caused by:
options: nitrogen dissolving in the body forming bubbles

questions.question: Which document contains first aid and survival information?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.14.1 Recall first aid and survival information contained in ERSA

questions.question: Myopia is:
options: black out after a high G force manoeuvre
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2    2.8.2(e) common visual problems, viz myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism, presbyopia

questions.question: You have a planned flight at altitude, however you have a head cold. What are you most at risk of encountering?
questions.explanation: Flying with a head cold greatly increases the risk of ear and sinus barotrauma due to blocked pressure equalization pathways.

questions.question: Which cells are used for vision in low level light?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.1 Outline the anatomy of the eye and its functioning during the day and night

questions.question: Which cells are most sensitive for vision in light and colour?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.1 Outline the anatomy of the eye and its functioning during the day and night

questions.question: On approach to land at an airport covered by haze the pilot will feel they are:
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.12(b) depth perception, for example, flying over water, snow , desert and other featureless terrain effect of fog; haze; dust

questions.question: Approximately how long does it take for night vision to restore after being exposed to light?
questions.explanation: It takes about 20–30 minutes for your eyes to fully adapt and restore night vision after exposure to bright light. When moving from bright to dark environments, your eyes need time to regenerate rhodopsin (the light-sensitive pigment in the rods).
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.2 State the factors that affect night vision and identify methods of 'dark adaption'

questions.question: After donating blood, how long to wait before being able to fly?
questions.explanation: After donating blood, you typically need to wait before flying due to the temporary reduction in blood volume and oxygen-carrying capacity.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.5.1 Explain the effect on flying after giving a blood donation

questions.question: Which is an example of a pull mode conversation?
Which is an example of a push mode conversation?
Empty field Myopia is the tendency for us to take up a resting focus position when there is nothing to focus on. The usual distance of resting focus position in front of the eyes is:
options: as far as the eye can see
questions.explanation: Empty field myopia is a tendency for the eyes to default to a near-focus in featureless environments, reducing a pilot's ability to see distant objects clearly. The eyes focus at a near distance (1-2 metres) instead of infinity when there are no visual cues, such as in haze, cloud, or at night.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.2(b) empty field myopia

questions.question: The average rate of time adjustment the body makes after crossing a number of time zones is:
questions.explanation: After crossing multiple time zones, circadian rhythm disruption occurs. The average rate at which the human body adjusts to new time zones is 1.5 hours per day

questions.question: A pilot who makes multiple accurate landings during a day but overshoots the last landing, what kind of error is this?
questions.explanation: This is a sporadic error, typically caused by factors like fatigue, distraction, or loss of concentration after repetitive tasks. A sporadic error occurs when a normally skilled individual makes an infrequent or occasional mistake, despite having performed the task correctly many times before.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.15.2(a) give an example of a committed error and how action could be taken to ensure safe flight

questions.question: Hypoxia is when:
options: An excess amount of carbon dioxide is in the blood
an excess amount of carbon monoxide is in the blood
an excess amount of oxygen fills the cockpit
an insufficient amount of oxygen and the body is unable to function at full capacity
questions.explanation: Hypoxia occurs when there isn't enough oxygen reaching body tissues, impairing physical and cognitive functions — a critical risk in aviation, especially at altitude.

questions.question: The duration of sensory memory for visual images is approximately:
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.1 Outline the anatomy of the eye and its functioning during the day and night

questions.question: A pilot may think that they are too low on approach when:
options: The runway is wider than usual
The runway is narrower than usual
The runway is longer than usual
There is moderate rain on the windscreen.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.12(d)(ii) width and slope of runway

questions.question: After takeoff on a very dark night with no visual cues, as pilot accelerates the aircraft he/she feels as if the aircraft is in a nose up attitude. This effect is also known as:
questions.explanation: On a dark night takeoff with no visual cues, the somatogravic illusion can trick the pilot into perceiving a nose-up pitch when accelerating.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.5(f) somatogravic illusion

questions.question: Which part of the eye is responsible for detecting light?
questions.explanation: Rods: Detect light intensity and are responsible for vision in low-light conditions.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.1 Outline the anatomy of the eye and its functioning during the day and night

questions.question: Which of the following conditions is most likely to lead to the leans?
options: pitching the nose of the aircraft rapidly
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.5(a) leans

questions.question: When must supplemental oxygen be carried?
questions.explanation: Under CASA regulations (CASR Part 91) the requirements for carrying and using supplemental oxygen are supplemental oxygen must be carried and used by crew above 10,000 ft
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2    2.13.1(f) list methods of combating various forms of hypoxia

questions.question: What level of sound exposure will cause permanent hearing loss?
questions.explanation: Continuous exposure to sounds ≥85 dB or any exposure to sounds >120 dB risks permanent hearing loss, especially without protection.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.5.3(a) hearing loss: long and short term

questions.question: Alcohol impairs pilot performance by affecting the:
Which of the following best describes the impact of alcohol when combined with altitude exposure?
questions.explanation: Alcohol consumption intensifies the effects of hypoxia at altitude, increasing the risk of impaired performance and decision-making in pilots. At altitude, the lower oxygen availability combined with alcohol’s depressive effect on the central nervous system significantly worsens hypoxia symptoms.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.13.1(d) list factors which may increase a persons susceptability to hypoxia

questions.question: Which type of drug is most associated with impairments in judgment, coordination, and reaction time when flying?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.4.4(c) opium based substances, for example codiene, heroin

questions.question: What is the most likely cause of the graveyard spin illusion?
options: Misleading inner ear signals after prolonged spin
questions.explanation: The graveyard spin illusion is most likely caused by the vestibular system in the inner ear, specifically due to the semicircular canals failing to detect continued rotation after a few seconds.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.5(e) graveyard spin illusion

questions.question: Which sensory system is primarily responsible for the graveyard spin illusion?
questions.explanation: The graveyard spin illusion is most likely caused by the vestibular system in the inner ear, specifically due to the semicircular canals failing to detect continued rotation after a few seconds.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.5(e) graveyard spin illusion

questions.question: What causes the somatogravic illusion in flight?
options: Rapid linear acceleration affecting the otolith organs
questions.explanation: The somatogravic illusion in flight is caused by linear acceleration or deceleration, which the otolith organs in the vestibular system misinterpret as a change in pitch attitude.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.5(f) somatogravic illusion

questions.question: During a rapid takeoff at night, a pilot experiencing the somatogravic illusion may incorrectly perceive:
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.5(f) somatogravic illusion

questions.question: A hazardous response to the somatogravic illusion during takeoff could be:
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.5(f) somatogravic illusion

questions.question: Which personality trait can negatively affect decision-making by causing a pilot to disregard advice or procedures?
Which of the following best describes a pilot who frequently takes unnecessary risks to prove their abilities?
A pilot who believes they cannot influence outcomes and therefore gives up easily is showing signs of:
questions.explanation: A pilot who believes they cannot influence outcomes and gives up easily is showing signs of resignation

questions.question: Which of the following is an ergonomic principle for cockpit design?
options: Place instruments randomly to avoid clutter
Group controls logically and within reach
Use dim lighting to reduce distraction

questions.question: Which model is commonly used to guide pilot decision-making under pressure?
In the DECIDE model, what does the “E” stand for in the final step?
questions.explanation: The final "E" means to evaluate the effectiveness of the actions taken.

questions.question: Which of the following is most likely to degrade a pilot's decision-making ability?
A pilot who fails to gather all available information before making a decision is likely neglecting which ADM step?
Which of the following is an example of a latent threat?
options: Incorrect switch labelling in the cockpit
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.15.3 Define 'threat' and give examples of threats

questions.question: A poorly designed checklist that leads to a missed item is an example of:
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.15.5 Explain how the use of checklist and standard operating procedures can prevent errors

questions.question: Why are latent threats particularly dangerous in aviation?
options: They are easy to detect but hard to avoid
They are rare but always catastrophic
They often remain unnoticed until they combine with other factors
questions.explanation: Latent threats are dangerous because they remain hidden until activated, increasing the risk of accidents through complex interactions with other factors.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.15.3 Define 'threat' and give examples of threats

questions.question: What type of colour vision deficiency is most common in males?
questions.explanation: The most common type of colour vision deficiency in males is red-green colour blindness, specifically Deuteranomaly (green-weakness).
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.2(d) colour vision in aviation

questions.question: How does the CASA medical standards address colour vision deficiencies in pilots?
options: Minor deficiencies may be acceptable with restrictions
Pilots with deficiencies must wear corrective lenses
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.8.2(d) colour vision in aviation

questions.question: Which body area is most commonly affected by trapped gases during ascent?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.4.1(a) recall the effect of changes in pressure on gasses trapped in the body cavities

questions.question: How long after consuming contaminated food do food poisoning symptoms typically appear?
questions.explanation: Symptoms usually develop within a few hours to 2 days, but the exact timing depends on the specific pathogen or toxin.

questions.question: Which action can help relieve trapped gas pain during flight?
Which symptom is most commonly associated with motion sickness?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.9.1 Describe the cause of motion sickness

questions.question: What causes motion sickness in pilots?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.9.1 Describe the cause of motion sickness

questions.question: What is the main physiological effect of positive G-forces on the pilot’s body?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.10.1(c) consciousness

questions.question: Negative G-forces primarily cause which effect?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2   2.10.1(c) consciousness

questions.question: How can effective crew resource management (CRM) assist in managing a UAS?
When first recognising an undesired aircraft state, what is the priority action?
options: Maintain aircraft control and delay recovery actions until stable
questions.explanation: The first and most critical action is to fly (stabilise) the aircraft before addressing other concerns.

questions.question: How can automation assist or hinder management of an undesired aircraft state?
options: Automation can mask symptoms, delaying pilot recognition
Automation is irrelevant to UAS management
Automation replaces the need for pilot intervention

questions.question: Why might pilots experience reduced thirst sensation during flight?
options: Cabin humidity is higher at altitude

questions.question: Why might pilots not notice dehydration symptoms during short flights?
questions.explanation: On short flights, dehydration signs may be subtle and masked by task focus, making them less noticeable to pilots.

questions.question: What is hyperopia?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2    2.8.2(e) common visual problems, viz myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism, presbyopia

questions.question: How can uncorrected hyperopia affect a pilot during flight?
options: Difficulty reading instruments at close range
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2    2.8.2(e) common visual problems, viz myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism, presbyopia

questions.question: How might uncorrected myopia affect a pilot during flight?
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2    2.8.2(e) common visual problems, viz myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism, presbyopia

questions.question: Which symptom might a pilot with uncorrected myopia experience?
options: Eye strain when reading close question
questions.explanation: Blurry distance vision is the primary symptom a pilot with uncorrected myopia would experience, which can impact flight safety.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2    2.8.2(e) common visual problems, viz myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism, presbyopia

questions.question: How does stress typically affect risk perception in pilots?
On a approach to long wide runway with terrain that slopes down, the pilot may feel that they are:
options: low on appoach and have a tendency to overshoot
high on appoach and have a tendency to overshoot
low on appoach and have a tendency to undershoot
high on appoach and have a tendency to undershoot
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.12(d)(ii) width and slope of runway

questions.question: A disorientated pilot should:
options: combine flight instrument indications with visual cues
pitch the nose up and re-orientated

questions.question: On a approach to short runway with terrain that slopes down, the pilot may feel that they are:
options: low on approach and have a tendency to overshoot
high on approach and have a tendency to overshoot
low on approach and have a tendency to undershoot
high on approach and have a tendency to undershoot
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.7.12(d)(ii) width and slope of runway

questions.question: During a climb, expanded gases are most likely to cause pain when it is trapped in:
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.4.1(a) recall the effect of changes in pressure on gases trapped in the body cavities

questions.question: What is hypertension?
questions.explanation: Hypertension is the medical term for high blood pressure—a condition where the force of blood pushing against the walls of the arteries is consistently too high.

questions.question: Which of the following best reflects a hidden danger of a shallow cockpit gradient?
Which approach is most effective in managing cockpit gradient during flight?
options: Assigning all decisions to the captain
Encouraging mutual respect and open communication
Limiting first officer input to non-critical phases

questions.question: What is a potential risk of a shallow cockpit gradient in a two-pilot operation?
options: The captain may ignore safety concerns
Crew may hesitate to question decisions
Confusion over leadership and decision-making responsibilities

questions.question: In which situation could a shallow cockpit gradient be least appropriate?
options: Routine line operations in stable weather

questions.question: Why is it important for flight crew to understand their own and each other’s personality types?
options: To optimise communication and workload management

questions.question: In a CRM conquestion, which trait might an extroverted first officer need to manage during a high-stress situation?
What is a potential strength of an introverted pilot under high workload conditions?
options: Remains calm and focused on internal processes

questions.question: What is hypertension, and which of the following is a likely contributing factor?
options: A condition where blood pressure is abnormally low
A state of increased intraocular pressure
Abnormally high blood pressure, often linked to stress, diet, or genetics
A temporary drop in blood pressure due to dehydration

questions.question: What is the EPT (Effective Perfromance Time) at 20,000ft?
questions.explanation: The Effective Performance Time (EPT), also known as Time of Useful Consciousness (TUC), at 20,000 feet is typically 5 - 12 minutes
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2    2.13.1(e) state the approximate time of useful conciousness (effective performance time: EPT) at 20,000, 25,000 and 30,000ft and list factors which effect EPT

questions.question: What is the EPT (Effective Perfromance Time) at 25,000ft?
questions.explanation: The Effective Performance Time (EPT), also known as Time of Useful Consciousness (TUC), at 25,000 feet is typically 3 - 5minutes
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2    2.13.1(e) state the approximate time of useful conciousness (effective performance time: EPT) at 20,000, 25,000 and 30,000ft and list factors which effect EPT

questions.question: Without supplementary oxygen, what is the EPT (Effective Perfromance Time) at 30,000ft?
questions.explanation: The Effective Performance Time (EPT), also known as Time of Useful Consciousness (TUC), at 30,000 feet is typically 1-2 minutes
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.2    2.13.1(e) state the approximate time of useful conciousness (effective performance time: EPT) at 20,000, 25,000 and 30,000ft and list factors which effect EPT

questions.question: According to CASA regulations, what is the recommended minimum “bottle-to-throttle” time (time between last alcohol intake and flying)?
Which symptom is commonly associated with hyperventilation?
options: Tingling or numbness in fingers and lips
questions.explanation: Low CO2 causes blood vessels to constrict, reducing blood flow to the brain and extremities, resulting in tingling sensations and numbness.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.3.2 Define hyperventilation and recall its causes

questions.question: What is hyperventilation?
options: Slow, shallow breathing leading to increased CO2 in the blood
Rapid, deep breathing causing excessive loss of carbon dioxide (CO2)
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation involves over-breathing, which reduces CO2 levels (hypocapnia) in the blood, leading to various physiological symptoms.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.3.2 Define hyperventilation and recall its causes

questions.question: What is an effective method to manage hyperventilation during flight?
options: Breathing into a paper bag or cupped hands to rebreathe CO2
questions.explanation: Rebreathing CO2 helps restore normal levels, alleviating symptoms caused by hyperventilation.
questions.kd: Unit 1.6.1   2.3.2 Define hyperventilation and recall its causes

questions.question: Which of the following is a common risk factor for developing hypertension?
questions.explanation: Excessive salt intake can cause the body to retain water, increasing blood volume and raising blood pressure.

questions.question: Which respiratory effect of smoking can impair a pilot’s performance at altitude?
options: Enhanced lung capacity at altitudes above 8,000ft
Improved ability to breathe at high altitude
Less susceptable to hypoxia at night
Chronic bronchitis and reduced lung function
questions.explanation: Smoking damages airways and lungs, leading to decreased respiratory efficiency and increased risk of hypoxia at altitude.
